en.extract:
The Saint Lucia warbler (Setophaga delicata) is a species of passerine bird belonging to the family Parulidae, the New World warblers. This species is endemic to Saint Lucia. It was formerly considered a subspecies of the Adelaide's warbler.


Taxonomy
The St Lucia warbler was first formally described as Dendroeca adelaidae delicata in 1883 by the American ornithologist Robert Ridgway with its type locality given as St Lucia. This species is now classified within the genus Setophaga which belongs to the family Parulidae, the wood warblers or New World warblers. The Saint Lucia warbler was considered to be a subspecies of Adelaide's warbler(S. adelaidae) of Puerto Rico, along with the Barbuda warbler (S. subita) but these are now regarded as separate species. The three species formerly known as Adelaide's warbler are classified in the "yellow-throated" species group, S. dominica, within Setophaga.


Etymology
The Saint Lucia warbler is classified in the genus Setophaga; this name means "moth eater" in Greek. The specific name, delicata, is Latin and means "dainty", "nice" or "delicate".


Description
The Saint Lucia warbler is a small bird with a length of 12.5 cm (4.9 in). The upperparts are bluish-grey and it has a yellow throat and breast, a yellow supercilium and a yellow crescent underneath the eye, both edged with black. The females are similar to the males but the black margin of the crown stripe is less obvious and there is less white in the tail. The song is variable, but always includes a series of fluty notes and frequently ends with two notes which are written as "which you". The call is a single loud chip note. 


Distribution and habitat
The Saint Lucia warbler is endemic to St Lucia in the Lesser Antilles where it is found in forests and in well wooded gardens.


References

The '''Saint Lucia warbler''' ('''''Setophaga delicata''''') is a species of [[passerine]] bird belonging to the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Parulidae]], the New World warblers. This species is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Saint Lucia]]. It was formerly considered a subspecies of the [[Adelaide's warbler]].

==Taxonomy==
The St Lucia warbler was first formally [[Species description|described]] as ''Dendroeca adelaidae delicata'' in 1883 by the American [[ornithologist]] [[Robert Ridgway]] with its [[Type locality (biology)|type locality]] given as St Lucia.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Robert Ridgway | journal=Proceedings of the United States National Museum | title=Description of a new warbler from the island of Santa Lucia, West Indies | volume=5 | issue=310 | pages=525–526 | publisher=Smithsonian Institution Press, [etc.] | date= 1883 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7321037 | doi=10.5479/si.00963801.5-310.521}}</ref> This species is now classified within the genus ''[[Setophaga]]'' which belongs to the family Parulidae, the wood warblers or New World warblers.<ref name = Avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=16 October 2025 }}</ref> The Saint Lucia warbler was considered to be a subspecies of [[Adelaide's warbler]](''S. adelaidae'') of [[Puerto Rico]], along with the [[Barbuda warbler]] (''S. subita'') but these are now regarded as separate species.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Staicer, Cynthia A. |date=October 1996 |title=Acoustical features of song categories of the Adelaide's Warbler (''Dendroica adelaidae'') |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v113n04/p0771-p0783.pdf |journal=The Auk |volume=113 |issue=4 |pages=771–783 |doi=10.2307/4088856 |jstor=4088856}}</ref> The three species formerly known as Adelaide's warbler are classified in the "[[yellow-throated warbler|yellow-throated]]" species group, S. ''dominica'', within ''Setophaga''.<ref name = Curson>{{cite book |author=Jon Curson |author2=David Quinn |name-list-style=and |author3=David Beadle |year=1994 |title=New World Warblers |series=Helm Identification Guides |publisher=Christopher Helm (Publishers) Ltd |pages=139–140 |isbn=0-7136-3932-6}}</ref>

==Etymology==
The Saint Lucia warbler is classified in the genus ''Setophaga''; this name means "moth eater" in Greek. The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]], ''delicata'', is Latin and means "dainty", "nice" or "delicate".<ref name =job>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A. | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url= https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher=Christopher Helm | location = London, United Kingdom | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | page =[https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n355 355][https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n131 131] }}</ref>

==Description==
The Saint Lucia warbler is a small bird with a length of {{cvt|12.5|cm}}. The upperparts are bluish-grey and it has a yellow throat and breast, a yellow [[supercilium]] and a yellow crescent underneath the eye, both edged with black. The females are similar to the males but the black margin of the crown stripe is less obvious and there is less white in the tail.<ref name = BWI>{{cite book |author=Herbert Raffaele |author2=James Wiley |author3=Orlando Garrido |author4=Allan Keith |author5=Janis Raffaele |year=2003 |title=Birds of the West Indies |pages=160–161 |series = Helm Field Guides |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0-7136-5419-6}}</ref> The song is variable, but always includes a series of fluty notes and frequently ends with two notes which are written as "which you". The call is a single loud chip note.<ref name = eBird>{{cite web |url=https://ebird.org/species/stlwar |title=Saint Lucia Warbler ''Setophaga delicata'' |access-date=13 July 2025 |publisher=[[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]] |work=[[eBird]]}}</ref> 

==Distribution and habitat==
The Saint Lucia warbler is endemic to St Lucia in the Lesser Antilles where it is found in forests and in well wooded gardens.<ref name = Drewitt>{{cite web |url=http://birdsofsaintlucia.com/the-st-lucia-warbler/ |title=The St Lucia Warbler |access-date=13 July 2025 |author=Ed Drewitt |date=7 November 2019 |work=Birds of St Lucia |publisher=Anse Chastenet}}</ref>
